metadata.summary: Structure design conversations where participants analyze work. Feedback: Observation → Principle → Impact → (Optional suggestion).

content.title: Design Critique
content.summary: Design critique is a structured conversation about a design where participants analyze work against defined goals and principles, providing specific and actionable feedback. Every piece of feedback must follow: Observation → Principle/Goal → Impact → (Optional suggestion). Feedback not tied to a stated goal is opinion, not critique.

why.p: Unstructured design reviews become opinion battles where personal preferences override user evidence. Structured critique produces specific, goal-anchored feedback that the designer can act on. The Observation → Principle → Impact chain forces reviewers to justify feedback, filtering taste from insight.

list.item: Pre-session preparation: generating a structured critique brief from design goals and constraints
Async critique: reviewing a design description against stated goals and producing structured feedback
Post-session synthesis: organizing critique notes into prioritized action items
Solo work: using an agent as a structured sounding board when no human reviewers are available
Training designers to give and receive goal-based feedback
Replacing human critique sessions — critique is partly a social alignment process that builds shared design language
When design goals are undefined — critique without objectives degrades to preference-based feedback
Final production polish decisions — agent cannot assess micro-interaction feel or animation timing
When stakeholder buy-in is the primary goal — human-led critique creates co-ownership

section.name: critique-vs-criticism
section.p: Critique: based on stated goals, specific and actionable, about the work, asks questions, improves design.
Criticism: based on personal preference, vague, about the designer, makes judgments, hurts feelings.

rule.statement: Every piece of feedback must be connected to a stated design goal or usability principle.
rule.rationale: Feedback not anchored to a goal is preference; preference-based feedback cannot be evaluated objectively and degrades into opinion battles.

section.name: roles
section.p: Presenter — shows work, states goals and constraints, asks for specific feedback type.
Facilitator — keeps discussion on track, ensures all voices are heard, redirects opinion to goals.
Participants — ask clarifying questions before giving feedback, connect observations to goals.
Note-taker — captures key feedback, questions raised, decisions made, action items.

rule.statement: The presenter decides what feedback to incorporate; it is their design, their call.
rule.rationale: Removing designer agency from critique sessions destroys ownership and produces resentful implementation; the presenter's authority over their work must be explicit.

section.name: stage-appropriate-feedback
section.p: Directional feedback (Is this the right approach?) — for exploration stage; many options open.
Refinement feedback (How can this be better?) — for iteration stage; one direction being developed.
Polish feedback (Is this ready to ship?) — for final stage; micro-decisions and completeness.

rule.statement: State the design stage before showing designs; polish feedback on exploration work is harmful.
rule.rationale: Polish feedback on early exploration locks in a direction prematurely and wastes time debating details that may be discarded.

section.name: five-step-process
section.p: Step 1: Set context — problem, user, constraints, stage, feedback type requested.
Step 2: Present the work systematically — explain decisions made, point out areas of uncertainty, do not over-apologize.
Step 3: Clarifying questions — participants ask to understand, not judge, before giving feedback.
Step 4: Give structured feedback — Observation → Principle/Goal → Impact → (Optional suggestion).
Step 5: Capture and prioritize — note-taker documents feedback, questions, decisions, and action items.

example.title: Well-formed feedback
example.note: Observation: "The submit button is below the fold." Principle: "Users frequently miss below-fold CTAs on mobile." Impact: "This could hurt conversion on small screens." Suggestion: "What if we added a sticky footer button?"

example.title: Preference-based feedback
example.note: "I don't like the colors." — preference statement with no observation, no principle, no impact. Not actionable.

rule.statement: Limit major concerns to five items maximum per critique session.
rule.rationale: Longer lists dilute priority and overwhelm the designer; five major concerns force prioritization and produce actionable output.

rule.statement: Ask clarifying questions before giving feedback; do not assume you understand the constraint space.
rule.rationale: Feedback based on misunderstood constraints wastes the session and erodes trust; clarification first prevents this.

rule.statement: Provide at least two observations about what is working well, not only concerns.
rule.rationale: Critique that identifies only problems gives the designer no signal about what to preserve; positive observations are as valuable as negative ones for iteration.

antipattern.description: No stated design goals before critique begins.
antipattern.reason: Feedback without criteria defaults to taste; the session degrades into preference-based debate with no resolution mechanism.

antipattern.description: Solutioning instead of critiquing — redesigning the product in the meeting.
antipattern.reason: The critique session is for analysis and feedback, not real-time redesign; solutioning prevents the designer from incorporating multiple feedback sources into their own solution.

antipattern.description: Presenter defending every point instead of listening.
antipattern.reason: Defensive presenter blocks feedback reception; thanking participants for honest feedback is the appropriate response during the session.

rule.statement: Provide a list of decisions already made (not open for discussion) when requesting agent critique.
rule.rationale: Agent may critique deliberate trade-offs it is unaware of; pre-specifying closed decisions prevents churn on settled questions.

rule.statement: Require agent to steelman the current design before critiquing when used for solo work.
rule.rationale: Steelmanning surfaces the designer's own rationale explicitly and produces more balanced critique output.

rule.statement: Agent critique output is a starting point for human review, not the final critique record.
rule.rationale: Agent cannot replicate the back-and-forth clarification dynamic of real critique, and it may contradict design system rules it is unaware of.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A Claude subagent receives: (1) a design brief (problem, user, constraints, stage), (2) design goals with success criteria, and (3) a description of the design (screen-by-screen walkthrough, element list, or annotated screenshot text). It outputs structured critique in the standardized format (Observation → Principle/Goal → Impact → Optional suggestion), organized by severity: Major concerns first, then Minor, then Positive observations. A second optional pass compresses the critique into a prioritized action item table for the presenter.

Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent to manage critique sessions as structured SDD tasks with input/output artifacts, or a general Claude subagent with reviewer role to generate the critique document from design description.

section.name: cli-tools-and-services
section.p: Figma REST API: Export frame/component tree as JSON for structured agent input.
jq: Parse Figma JSON exports to extract element names, types, and hierarchy.
Loom CLI: Share screen recordings of prototype walkthroughs for async critique (web interface).
Playwright: Screenshot specific flows in a live prototype for structured agent review.
Figma (SaaS, partial REST): Export frame structure; agent reads element tree but not rendered visuals without vision.
Loom (SaaS): Async video critique; useful for human reviewers, not for agent input.
Notion (SaaS, API): Store critique sessions, action items, and decisions; agent writes structured output via API.
Linear (SaaS, API): Convert critique action items directly to design tickets.
GitHub Discussions (API): Async critique threads for design work stored in repos; agent can post/parse via GitHub API.

list.item: Always include the design stage in the critique request — agent feedback calibrated for "Exploration" looks different from "Polish"
Provide stated design goals with measurable success criteria; without them, agent critique defaults to generic heuristics
Require the agent to limit feedback to 5 major concerns maximum — longer lists dilute priority and overwhelm the designer
Run the Observation → Principle → Impact chain strictly; reject agent outputs that skip the principle/goal link
Use agent critique output as a starting point for human review, not as the final critique record
For solo work, ask agent to steelman the current design before critiquing — this surfaces the designer's own rationale explicitly

template.purpose: Full critique session doc: context, design goals, notes, action items, decisions
Observation → Principle → Impact → Suggestion format with worked example
